findings.summary: requests is a core runtime dependency but is used exclusively for Ollama HTTP calls in the LLM layer
evidence: pyproject.toml line 44: 'requests>=2.31.0' listed as a top-level runtime dependency
infrastructure/llm/core/client.py line 21: 'import requests' — the only non-test consumer of the requests library at module level

The ollama dependency group (pyproject.toml line 61) is optional, but requests which supports it is listed as required — requests is heavy (brings urllib3, certifi, charset-normalizer, idna) for what is essentially one HTTP endpoint call
findings.suggestion: Move 'requests' into the optional 'llm' dependency group alongside 'ollama'. The LLM client already gracefully degrades (PROMPT_LOADER_AVAILABLE flag), so the import can be made conditional or requests replaced with stdlib urllib for simple POST calls.

findings.summary: pillow is a core runtime dependency but its use appears limited to optional steganography and image overlay features
evidence: pyproject.toml line 45: 'pillow>=10.0.0' listed as a required runtime dependency
pyproject.toml line 72: 'qrcode[pil]>=8.2' already depends on pillow for the optional steganography group — pillow is pulled in regardless
pillow is a large binary wheel (~4MB) pulled in unconditionally even for users who never use figure overlays or steganography
findings.suggestion: Evaluate whether pillow is genuinely needed on all install paths. If its only required use is in optional overlay/steganography code, move it to the 'steganography' optional group and guard the import sites with try/except.

findings.summary: scipy is listed as an 'optional' package in environment.py default checks but is not declared in pyproject.toml at all
evidence: infrastructure/core/environment.py lines 90-91: optional_packages = ['scipy'] — checked and warned about during pipeline setup
pyproject.toml has no scipy entry in dependencies or any dependency group
This creates a latent dependency that users are warned about but have no declarative way to install via uv
findings.suggestion: Either add scipy as an optional dependency group in pyproject.toml (e.g. '[dependency-groups] scientific = ["scipy"]') or remove it from the environment check if it is no longer needed.

findings.summary: Several high-value functions in environment.py have no test coverage
evidence: test_environment.py only imports and tests check_python_version and check_dependencies

Functions with no test coverage in environment.py: install_missing_packages (lines 121-197), setup_directories (lines 261-288), get_subprocess_env (lines 381-402), validate_interpreter (lines 338-378), validate_uv_sync_result (lines 497-527), validate_directory_structure (lines 530-558)
setup_directories and validate_directory_structure are called by the pipeline on every run — these are critical paths
test_environment.py TestEnvironmentSetupIntegration tests mkdir via tmp_path directly, not via the actual setup_directories() function
findings.suggestion: Add tests for setup_directories(tmp_path, 'test_project'), validate_directory_structure(tmp_path, 'test_project'), and get_subprocess_env() using real temp directories. validate_uv_sync_result can be tested by creating .venv and uv.lock files in tmp_path.

findings.summary: projects/code_project/tests/ contains only one test file covering one src module
evidence: Glob of projects/code_project/tests/ returns only: __init__.py, conftest.py, test_optimizer.py
test_optimizer.py imports from src.optimizer only — no other src modules are tested
If code_project/src/ has more than one module, those modules have zero coverage from the project test suite
findings.suggestion: Audit projects/code_project/src/ for additional modules and add corresponding test files to reach the stated 90% project coverage requirement.

findings.summary: test_health_check.py uses time.sleep and asserts timestamp ordering, creating a fragile timing-dependent test
evidence: test_health_check.py line 117: time.sleep(0.01)
Line 118-119: assert status2['timestamp'] >= status1['timestamp'] — the assertion uses >= not > meaning the sleep is required to distinguish timestamps but the assertion is too weak to require it
findings.suggestion: The test intent (fresh timestamps per call) is valid but relies on wall-clock time. Assert that timestamp is a float close to time.time() instead, or remove the sleep and only assert the type is float > 0.

findings.summary: Infrastructure-dependent test classes in test_optimizer.py use pytest.skip() when infrastructure is unavailable, silently dropping coverage
evidence: Lines 20-35: INFRASTRUCTURE_AVAILABLE flag set via try/except import from scripts/optimization_analysis.py
Lines 440-442, 489-491, 538-540: @pytest.mark.skipif(not INFRASTRUCTURE_AVAILABLE, ...) decorates three test classes
Lines 451-466, 469-484, 495-515, etc.: individual test bodies also call pytest.skip() if result_path is None — double-guarded skips
This means TestStabilityAnalysis, TestPerformanceBenchmarking, TestAnalysisDashboard can all silently skip in CI without any failure signal
findings.suggestion: Replace soft INFRASTRUCTURE_AVAILABLE skip with a hard fixture that asserts the scripts are importable. If the scripts are part of the project, they should always be importable in the test environment and failures should be test failures, not skips.

findings.summary: infrastructure/validation/ contains 22 Python files with mixed concerns — document scanning, link checking, PDF validation, and audit orchestration are all co-located
evidence: Glob of infrastructure/validation/*.py returns 22 files: known_exceptions.py, mock_validator.py, figure_validator.py, link_validator.py, output_validator.py, doc_scanner.py, doc_models.py, audit_orchestrator.py, issue_categorizer.py, doc_accuracy.py, doc_discovery.py, repo_scanner.py, doc_completeness.py, doc_quality.py, markdown_validator.py, check_links.py, __init__.py, cli.py, pdf_validator.py, integrity.py, validate_pdf_cli.py, validate_markdown_cli.py

The directory mixes at least 4 distinct sub-concerns: (1) PDF/markdown validation, (2) document scanning/accuracy (doc_scanner, doc_accuracy, doc_completeness, doc_quality, doc_models, doc_discovery), (3) link checking (link_validator, check_links, check_links.py), (4) audit orchestration (audit_orchestrator, issue_categorizer, known_exceptions, repo_scanner)
infrastructure/validation/__init__.py re-exports 28 symbols, making the public surface opaque
findings.suggestion: Split into sub-packages: validation/document/ (doc_scanner, doc_models, doc_accuracy, doc_completeness, doc_quality, doc_discovery), validation/links/ (link_validator, check_links), validation/audit/ (audit_orchestrator, issue_categorizer, known_exceptions, repo_scanner). This reduces the flat file count from 22 to ~5 per sub-package.

findings.summary: infrastructure/validation/mock_validator.py has a name that implies it provides mock validation objects, but it actually enforces a no-mocks policy
evidence: mock_validator.py line 1-5: module docstring says 'Validates that no mock objects or frameworks are used in tests, enforcing the No Mocks Policy'
The file name 'mock_validator' reads as 'a validator that is a mock' or 'provides mock validators', not 'validates that mocks are absent'
The module is not exported from infrastructure/validation/__init__.py — it is a navigation dead-end for anyone browsing the package surface
findings.suggestion: Rename to no_mock_enforcer.py or mock_policy_checker.py to make the intent explicit. Also either add it to __init__.py exports or move it to a test utilities location since it scans test directories.

findings.summary: infrastructure/core/ contains 27 Python files with logging split across 5 separate modules
evidence: Glob of infrastructure/core/*.py returns 27 files
Logging concern is fragmented across: logging_utils.py, logging_constants.py, logging_formatters.py, logging_helpers.py, logging_progress.py — 5 files for one cross-cutting concern
Additional peripheral concerns: menu.py, cli.py, config_cli.py are CLI entry-points co-located with core utilities like retry.py, security.py, health_check.py
findings.suggestion: Consolidate logging sub-modules under infrastructure/core/logging/ sub-package. Move CLI-adjacent files (menu.py, cli.py, config_cli.py) to infrastructure/cli/ to reduce core's scope.

findings.summary: infrastructure/validation/mock_validator.py imports get_logger inside an exception handler rather than at module level
evidence: mock_validator.py lines 69-72: inside the except block of validate_no_mocks(), 'from infrastructure.core.logging_utils import get_logger' and 'logger = get_logger(__name__)' are called on every exception
This means a new logger is instantiated on every file-read failure, and the import is re-executed each time the except branch runs
Every other infrastructure module imports get_logger at module level (e.g. config_loader.py line 15, environment.py line 45)
findings.suggestion: Move 'from infrastructure.core.logging_utils import get_logger' and 'logger = get_logger(__name__)' to module level, consistent with all other infrastructure modules.

findings.summary: infrastructure/reporting/__init__.py imports get_logger inside the generate_multi_project_report function body
evidence: reporting/__init__.py line 80: 'from infrastructure.core.logging_utils import get_logger' is inside the generate_multi_project_report() function body
Line 82: 'logger = get_logger(__name__)' — logger is created inside the function on every call
The module already has top-level imports from 7+ submodules; adding a module-level logger would be consistent
findings.suggestion: Move the get_logger import and logger instantiation to module level in reporting/__init__.py, matching the pattern used by all infrastructure submodules.

findings.summary: infrastructure/core/config_loader.py has a bare except Exception that discards the exception type after already catching specific exceptions
evidence: config_loader.py lines 157-162: load_config() catches '(FileNotFoundError, PermissionError, yaml.YAMLError)' specifically, then falls through to 'except Exception: logger.warning(...); return None'

The broad except on line 159 swallows any unexpected exception (e.g. MemoryError, KeyboardInterrupt caught transitively) with only a warning log — the caller gets None with no signal about what went wrong
The warning message at line 161 does not include the exception itself: 'logger.warning(f"Unexpected error loading config from {config_path}")' — exception is silently dropped
findings.suggestion: Change the broad except to log the exception: 'logger.warning(f"Unexpected error loading config from {config_path}: {e}", exc_info=True)'. Consider narrowing to 'except (OSError, ValueError)' if those are the only realistic unexpected cases.

findings.summary: config_loader.get_testing_config() mixes walrus-operator style for env vars with explicit if/else for config file values in consecutive code blocks
evidence: Lines 432-442: env var extraction uses 'if env_infra := os.environ.get(...)' walrus pattern
Lines 452-461: config file extraction uses '(v := _safe_int_from_dict(...)) is not None' walrus pattern inside if-statements with explicit flag checks

Line 458-459: 'if not env_set_infra and (v := _safe_int_from_dict(testing_cfg, "infra_coverage_threshold")) is not None:' — the compound boolean with walrus and a boolean guard on the same line is difficult to parse at a glance
The two env-var boolean flags (env_set_infra, env_set_project) on lines 444-445 are set after the parsing loop rather than before, forcing the reader to mentally separate read from use
findings.suggestion: Extract env-var priority logic into a small helper that returns the resolved threshold value given env-var name, config dict key, and default. This would flatten the compound conditionals and make the priority chain readable in sequence.
